output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.5": 1.0, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Critique of the paragraph (really a multi-paragraph section). One-line summary: the section's biggest problem is ordering — the headline result and the pipeline description are split apart by a digression about disagreement-as-novelty, and several quantitative claims need tightening.

**Ordering is inverted.** The standard arc for a results-oriented section is: problem → prior work → our system → evaluation setup → headline result → secondary findings → interpretation. As written, the disagreement-as-novelty finding (paragraph 2) appears before the system has been described, before the evaluation has been defined, and before the reader knows what the "three member classifiers" even are. Move paragraph 2 to after the macro-F1 result. Suggested order: P1 (motivation/prior work) → P3 (RubinTriage description) → P4 (data) → P5 (headline result) → P2 (disagreement finding) → P6 (interpretation).

**The disagreement-as-novelty claim is the most interesting result but the least supported.** "63% … were later confirmed as rare or peculiar" raises immediate questions the text does not answer:
- 63% of how many? Give N. If this is 12/19, it is anecdote, not finding.
- What is the base rate of rare/peculiar transients in the validation set? 63% is only striking if the base rate is, say, 2%. State the lift.
- How is "maximally disagreed" defined? Top-1 disagreement across all three? Entropy threshold? Specify.
- "Later confirmed" by whom — spectroscopic follow-up, TNS reclassification, expert review?

Without these, the claim is unfalsifiable and a reviewer will flag it.

**Headline-result claims need tightening.**
- "outperforming each individual classifier by 4–7 F1 points" — give the three numbers, not a range. A range hides whether the gain is uniform or driven by one weak member.
- "matching the published Alerce broker on the four classes Alerce reports" — name the four classes and give both F1 numbers. "Matching" is doing a lot of work; ±how much?
- "individual classifiers collapse to 0.3 F1 … ensemble reaches 0.55" — for which class? TDEs and kilonovae presumably behave very differently given their sample sizes. Split them.
- 5,300 test alerts across 14 classes implies some classes have tens of examples; F1 on those is high-variance. Report confidence intervals or bootstrap.

**Prior work is thin.** Möller et al. and Sánchez-Sáez et al. are cited without years or specific claims; readers cannot tell which papers. More importantly, Alerce, Fink, ANTARES, and Lasair are the obvious comparison brokers — only Alerce is mentioned, and only on four classes. Either justify the narrow comparison or broaden it. SuperNNova (Möller & de Boissière) and the RAPID classifier (Muthukrishna et al.) are also standard baselines for this exact problem and should at least be acknowledged.

**The closing interpretation overreaches slightly.** "ensemble design … should explicitly maximize representational diversity rather than only voting accuracy" is a defensible suggestion, but the evidence presented is a single ensemble with three deliberately diverse members — there is no ablation showing that diversity (rather than just having three reasonable models) is what drove the gain. Either soften ("is consistent with") or add an ablation where members are replaced with architecturally similar variants.

**Minor prose / accuracy issues.**
- "order 10^6 transient candidates per night" — ZTF is ~10^5–10^6, Rubin projected ~10^7. The combined phrasing blurs them; split.
- "operate within minutes of detection" — Rubin's alert-stream latency target is 60 seconds; "minutes" understates the constraint.
- "kilonovae candidates" — be consistent (you use "kilonovae" earlier).
- "stacked logistic meta-learner trained on a held-out spectroscopic sample" — is this the same held-out sample as the 5,300-alert test set? If so, you have a leakage problem; if not, say so explicitly.
- "calcium-rich gap transients, AT2018cow analogues, or interacting Type IIn SNe" — Type IIn are not rare; they are a known class. The list mixes "rare" with "peculiar but not rare." Rephrase.
- "posterior distributions" — for a gradient-boosted tree these are not posteriors in any Bayesian sense; "class probability outputs" is more accurate.

Uncertainty disclosure: claims about Rubin alert volume (~10^7/night) and 60-second latency target reflect pre-2026 published Rubin/LSST plans; verify against the current DPS documentation. The specific Möller/Sánchez-Sáez/Muthukrishna paper attributions are from memory and should be checked before citing.
